title: SAA-C03 Daily Mock
subtitle: Day 4 — IAM Identity Policies, Users & ARNs, Groups, and Roles

questions.stem: An IAM user is granted an action by one attached policy, but another attached policy contains an explicit Deny for that same action. What is the effective result when the user attempts it?

options.text: The action is denied, because an explicit Deny overrides any Allow.
options.why: Correct — in policy evaluation an explicit Deny always wins over any Allow.

options.text: The action is allowed, because a specific Allow takes priority over a Deny.
options.why: Wrong — Allow does not beat Deny; an explicit Deny overrides it.

options.text: The action is allowed, because the most recently attached policy is the one that wins.
options.why: Wrong — evaluation is not based on attachment order; an explicit Deny still applies.

options.text: The action is denied only when every attached policy happens to deny it.
options.why: Wrong — a single explicit Deny is enough; all policies need not agree.

questions.stem: Within an identity policy, what does a single statement primarily specify?

options.text: An Effect (Allow or Deny) along with the Actions and the Resources it applies to.
options.why: Correct — a statement pairs an Effect with the Actions and Resources (and optional conditions).

options.text: The specific username and password combination the policy applies to whenever a user signs in.
options.why: Wrong — policies reference identities and permissions, not a username/password pair.

options.text: The billing account that will be charged whenever the listed actions run.
options.why: Wrong — statements do not define billing; they define permissions.

options.text: The Region and Availability Zone in which the policy becomes active.
options.why: Wrong — IAM is global and statements are not scoped to an AZ like this.

questions.stem: A team wants the same set of permissions applied to many users and roles, defined once and reused everywhere. Which approach fits BEST?

options.text: Attach a single managed policy to each identity that needs those permissions.
options.why: Correct — a managed policy is authored once and attached to many identities, which is exactly this need.

options.text: Write the same inline policy separately on each individual identity that needs it.
options.why: Wrong — inline copies work but are not reusable or centrally managed, so they are inferior here.

options.text: Give every identity full administrator access so permissions never need to be managed at all.
options.why: Wrong — this violates least privilege and does not target the specific permission set.

options.text: Store the permissions as an object in S3 and link each identity to that file.
options.why: Wrong — S3 objects are not IAM policies and cannot grant permissions to identities.

questions.stem: A company with hundreds of thousands of employees wants them all to access AWS. Why is creating an individual IAM user for each employee the WRONG approach?

options.text: IAM suits a limited number of users per account, so at that scale you use federation or roles instead.
options.why: Correct — IAM has a per-account user ceiling (around 5,000), so large populations use federation/roles.

options.text: IAM users are billed individually every month, which at that scale would make maintaining all of them far too costly to justify.
options.why: Wrong — IAM users are not charged per user; cost is not the reason.

options.text: IAM users can only be created by the account root user, one user at a time.
options.why: Wrong — any sufficiently permissioned identity can create users, and not one at a time.

options.text: IAM users cannot be assigned passwords, so the employees would be unable to sign in.
options.why: Wrong — IAM users can have console passwords; this is simply false.

questions.stem: What is the purpose of an Amazon Resource Name (ARN)?

options.text: To uniquely identify a specific AWS resource, including across accounts and Regions.
options.why: Correct — an ARN is a globally unique identifier that pinpoints one specific resource.

options.text: To hold the secret access key that is used to authenticate that resource's API calls.
options.why: Wrong — an ARN is an identifier, not a credential; it stores no secret key.

options.text: To set the monthly spending limit that should be applied to a particular resource.
options.why: Wrong — ARNs do not control budgets or spending limits.

options.text: To define the network firewall rules that are meant to protect a given resource.
options.why: Wrong — firewall rules are unrelated; an ARN just names the resource.

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about IAM groups are correct? (Select TWO.)

options.text: A single IAM user can be a member of more than one group at the same time.
options.why: Correct — users can belong to multiple groups simultaneously.

options.text: A group has no credentials of its own and cannot be referenced as a principal.
options.why: Correct — groups are management containers with no credentials and are not principals.

options.text: Groups can be nested inside other groups to build a permission hierarchy.
options.why: Wrong — IAM groups cannot be nested.

options.text: You can sign in to the console directly as a group using shared group credentials.
options.why: Wrong — groups have no credentials and cannot be signed into.

options.text: Identity policies can be attached to individual users but never to a group.
options.why: Wrong — attaching policies to groups is a primary reason groups exist.

questions.stem: An IAM role has two policy documents associated with it. What do those two documents control?

options.text: A trust policy for who may assume the role, and a permissions policy for what it can do.
options.why: Correct — the trust policy defines who can assume the role; the permissions policy defines its access.

options.text: A trust policy that lists the role's long-term access keys, plus a separate billing policy.
options.why: Wrong — roles use temporary credentials, not stored long-term keys, and there is no billing policy.

options.text: Two permissions policies that are merged together to double the role's allowed actions.
options.why: Wrong — a role does not duplicate permissions policies to multiply access.

options.text: A password policy and an MFA policy that together secure the role's console sign-in.
options.why: Wrong — roles are assumed, not signed into with a password, so this does not apply.

questions.stem: When a trusted entity assumes an IAM role, how does it receive permission to act?

options.text: It is issued temporary security credentials that expire after a limited time.
options.why: Correct — assuming a role returns short-lived, automatically expiring credentials.

options.text: It is handed the role's permanent access keys to store and reuse indefinitely.
options.why: Wrong — roles provide temporary credentials, not permanent stored keys.

options.text: It must sign in with the role's own username and password on each attempt.
options.why: Wrong — roles have no username/password; they are assumed, not logged into.

options.text: It permanently inherits the account root user's credentials for that session.
options.why: Wrong — assuming a role never grants root user credentials.

questions.stem: An application running on an EC2 instance needs to read data from AWS services. What is the recommended way to give it that access?

options.text: Attach an IAM role to the instance so the application uses temporary credentials.
options.why: Correct — an instance role supplies rotating temporary credentials with no secrets stored on the instance.

options.text: Create an IAM user and hard-code its long-term access keys into the application config.
options.why: Wrong — embedding long-term keys works but is insecure and hard to rotate, so it is not recommended.

options.text: Use the account root user's access keys so that every service is reachable.
options.why: Wrong — root credentials must never be used by applications.

options.text: Store the keys in an S3 bucket and have the application download them at startup.
options.why: Wrong — this still relies on long-term keys and adds risk rather than using a role.

questions.stem: In which TWO situations is using an IAM role the appropriate choice? (Select TWO.)

options.text: An AWS service needs permissions to perform actions on your behalf.
options.why: Correct — granting an AWS service access via an assumed role is a primary use of roles.

options.text: Users from an external identity provider need temporary access to AWS.
options.why: Correct — federated/external identities assume a role for temporary access.

options.text: You want to embed a set of permanent credentials directly inside application code.
options.why: Wrong — roles exist to avoid embedding long-term credentials, not to enable it.

options.text: You need to grant the account root user some additional administrative power.
options.why: Wrong — root already has full power and is not extended by roles.

options.text: You want a group to be able to sign in to the console with a single login.
options.why: Wrong — groups cannot sign in; this is not a role use case.

questions.stem: An identity (permissions) policy grants or denies permissions by being attached to what?

options.text: An IAM identity such as a user, a group, or a role.
options.why: Correct — identity policies attach to IAM identities: users, groups, and roles.

options.text: A specific resource, such as an S3 bucket, which then carries the policy itself.
options.why: Wrong — that describes a resource policy, which attaches to the resource, not an identity policy.

options.text: The Region in which the granted permissions are meant to apply.
options.why: Wrong — identity policies are not attached to a Region.

options.text: The access key string that the identity uses when it authenticates.
options.why: Wrong — policies attach to the identity, not to its access key.

questions.stem: A workload needs a volume to install an operating system and a database engine onto, with low-latency block access. Why is S3 not the right service for this?

options.text: S3 is object storage and does not present the block volume an OS and database need.
options.why: Correct — S3 is object storage; it cannot act as a block volume to boot an OS or run a database on.

options.text: S3 can only store files smaller than a few kilobytes, which is too small for this.
options.why: Wrong — S3 objects can be very large (up to 5 TB); size is not the issue.

options.text: S3 forces all stored data to be encrypted, which a database engine cannot use.
options.why: Wrong — encryption is not what makes S3 unsuitable here; the storage type is.

options.text: S3 is confined to just a single Availability Zone, which would make it far too fragile to run a database on.
options.why: Wrong — S3 is Region-resilient across AZs; this statement is simply false.

questions.stem: An operations team needs memory utilization from inside several EC2 instances to show up in CloudWatch, but it isn't appearing. What is the reason and fix?

options.text: Memory is an in-OS metric, so the CloudWatch agent must be installed to publish it.
options.why: Correct — memory lives inside the guest OS, so the CloudWatch agent is required to send it.

options.text: Memory should appear on its own, so the instances just need a reboot to report it.
options.why: Wrong — rebooting does not add in-OS metrics; the agent is needed.

options.text: Memory only shows up once a CloudWatch alarm has been created for the instances.
options.why: Wrong — alarms watch existing metrics; they do not cause memory metrics to appear.

options.text: Memory is only available after detailed monitoring has been enabled on the instances.
options.why: Wrong — detailed monitoring only changes frequency, not which metrics exist.
